sentence: Vayse meu corachón de mib.
ipa: ˈbajse ˈmew koɾaˈtʃon ˈde ˈmib
gloss_en: My heart goes away from me.
notes: The most famous Andalusi Romance kharja (H9, García Gómez series), a Christian woman's voice closing an Arabic muwaššaḥ; corachón [koɾaˈtʃon] < cor + augmentative, with ⟨ch⟩ [tʃ] and no tonic diphthong (barbato_minervini2024).

sentence: Ya Rab, ¿sí me tornarad?
ipa: ja ˈrabb ˈsi me toɾnaˈɾad
gloss_en: O Lord, will he come back to me?
notes: Second line of the same kharja; initial ⟨R⟩ trill [r] in the Arabic loan-vocative Rab, verbal -ad future (tornarad) reconstructed per barbato_minervini2024. Blind-judge arbitration: Arabic vocative ya is the glide [j] (not the ge/j- palatal) and loan rabb keeps its Andalusi Arabic geminate; clitic me unstressed (barbato_minervini2024).

sentence: Tan mal meu doler li-l-ḥabib.
ipa: ˈtan ˈmal ˈmew doˈleɾ lilħaˈbib
gloss_en: So sore is my pain for the beloved.
notes: Third line; Andalusi Arabic adstrate ḥabib 'beloved' with ⟨ḥ⟩ [ħ] and the Arabic article li-l-, embedded in Romance syntax (barbato_minervini2024; adstrate per vicente2020).

sentence: Enfermo yed, ¿kuánd sanarad?
ipa: eˈnfeɾmo ˈjed kuˈand sanaˈɾad
gloss_en: He is sick; when will he get well?
notes: Fourth line; yed 'is' < Lat. est, apocopated kuánd 'when' (final-vowel loss, barbato_minervini2024:160), conserved voiceless -t- absent here but sanarad keeps -d < -t future ending.

sentence: Ya mamma, meu al-ḥabib vayse.
ipa: ja ˈmamma ˈmew alħaˈbib ˈbajse
gloss_en: O mother, my beloved goes away.
notes: Opening of kharja H14 (mama, meu, al-ḥabib attested there); non-diphthongized meu and mamma with geminate, Arabic article al- before ḥabib (barbato_minervini2024:158).

sentence: Garid vos, ay yermanellas, kómo kontenir meu male.
ipa: ɡaˈɾid ˈbos ˈaj ɟeɾmaneˈʎas ˈkomo konteˈniɾ ˈmew ˈmale
gloss_en: Tell me, o little sisters, how to contain my pain.
notes: yermanellas 'little sisters' (H4/H46, < germana + -ella) with ⟨y⟩ [ʝ] for Lat. ge- and diminutive -ella; male 'badly/pain' non-apocopated (barbato_minervini2024:160-161). ge/j- onset corrected to the stop [ɟ]: the early result of these bases, written yāʾ/yōd, is /ɟ/ (barbato_minervini2024:446-452 on yana, yermanellas, yenesta).

sentence: ¿Qué faré yo, o qué serad de mib?
ipa: ˈke faˈɾe ˈjo ˈo ˈke seˈɾad ˈde ˈmib
gloss_en: What shall I do, or what will become of me?
notes: Kharja of the 'gar que fareyu' type; faré future, serad < *serat, mib 'me' (< mihi) with final apocope; ⟨y⟩ [ʝ] onset in yo (barbato_minervini2024).

sentence: ¡Tant amare, tant amare, ḥabib, tant amare!
ipa: ˈtant aˈmaɾe ˈtant aˈmaɾe ħaˈbib ˈtant aˈmaɾe
gloss_en: So much loving, so much loving, beloved, so much loving!
notes: The famous anaphoric kharja; amare < amare(m) infinitive/gerundial, ḥabib adstrate loan [ħaˈbib]; vowel-final tant' before amare is an elision sandhi site (barbato_minervini2024).

sentence: Non te tolgas de mib, ya ḥabibi.
ipa: ˈnon ˈte ˈtolɡas ˈde ˈmib ja ħaˈbibi
gloss_en: Do not take yourself from me, o my beloved.
notes: tolgas 'you take away' (subjunctive), preverbal negator non; ḥabibi with the Arabic 1sg possessive -i (barbato_minervini2024; corriente1997).

sentence: Welyos nidios ke tan mal dolen.
ipa: weˈʎos niˈdios ˈke ˈtan ˈmal doˈlen
gloss_en: Bright eyes that hurt so badly.
notes: welyos 'eyes' (H18) — a metaphonic diphthong < oculos with -LY- > [ʎ], one of the rare Andalusi Romance diphthongs (barbato_minervini2024:159); dolen < dolent.

sentence: Filyo de la bokella dolçe.
ipa: ˈfiʎo ˈde la boˈkeʎa ˈdoltse
gloss_en: Son of the sweet little mouth.
notes: filyo 'son' (H1) with F- retained [f] and -LY- > [ʎ] (barbato_minervini2024:160-161); bokella 'little mouth' (A11) with conserved voiceless -k- [k] (galmes1983:92-97).

sentence: La farina bona yed en la yana.
ipa: la faˈɾina ˈbona ˈjed en la ˈɟana
gloss_en: The good flour is at the door.
notes: F- retention farina (not Castilian harina, barbato_minervini2024:160); bona non-diphthongized; yana 'door' < janua with ge/j > [ʝ] written ⟨y⟩ (galmes1983:81-82). yed < est keeps the diphthong-onset glide [j] (no consonant cluster arises word-initially, barbato_minervini2024:281 n.10); yana < janua takes the ge/j- stop [ɟ].

sentence: Matar me kere el amor.
ipa: maˈtaɾ me ˈkeɾe el aˈmoɾ
gloss_en: Love wants to kill me.
notes: matar 'to kill' (A26) with conserved intervocalic voiceless -t- [t], the archaic Ibero-Romance trait (barbato_minervini2024:161; galmes1983:92-97); vowel junction me-kere / el-amor sandhi. Blind-judge arbitration: clitics me/el unstressed; the close-mid [e] in kere is the set's convention — Latin Ĕ quality is under-differentiated by the romanized layer and left as a lead.

sentence: Bene serad si tornarad meu senyor.
ipa: ˈbene seˈɾad ˈsi toɾnaˈɾad ˈmew seˈɲoɾ
gloss_en: It will be well if my lord returns.
notes: bene 'well' (H1) — no diphthongization of Ĕ (not *biene, barbato_minervini2024:158); senyor with ⟨ny⟩ [ɲ] palatal nasal; -d future forms.

sentence: Sabes ke meu corachón es tuyo.
ipa: ˈsabes ˈke ˈmew koɾaˈtʃon ˈes ˈtujo
gloss_en: You know my heart is yours.
notes: sabes with Lat. -p- > ⟨b⟩ [b] (Arabic script lacked /p/, barbato_minervini2024:161); corachón [tʃ]; vowel-final sabes-ke and corachón junctions.

sentence: Ay, mano, meu ḥabib enfermo yed.
ipa: ˈaj ˈmano ˈmew ħaˈbib eˈnfeɾmo ˈjed
gloss_en: Alas, brother, my beloved is sick.
notes: kharja lament register; mano < germano address; enfermo non-diphthongized [eˈnfeɾmo]; adstrate ḥabib (barbato_minervini2024; vicente2020).

sentence: ¿Kómo vivir sin el meu amor?
ipa: ˈkomo biˈbiɾ ˈsin el ˈmew aˈmoɾ
gloss_en: How to live without my love?
notes: kómo with conserved -m-; sin el / meu amor vowel-junction sandhi; amor oxytone [aˈmoɾ] final-stress on a consonant (barbato_minervini2024:159-160).

sentence: Rrey de los cielos, sana meu male.
ipa: ˈrej ˈde ˈlos tʃiˈelos ˈsana ˈmew ˈmale
gloss_en: King of the heavens, heal my pain.
notes: geminate ⟨rr⟩ trill [r] in Rrey; devotional register; male non-apocopated; sana imperative — coverage over the trill and imperative axes (barbato_minervini2024).

sentence: Yenesta e yunko creçen en la vera.
ipa: ɟeˈnesta e ˈɟunko ˈkɾetsen en la ˈbeɾa
gloss_en: Broom and reed grow by the bank.
notes: botanical lexicon from Abulḫayr: yenesta < genesta and yunko < juncu, both with ge/j > [ʝ] written ⟨y⟩ (barbato_minervini2024:161; galmes1983:81-82); creçen with ⟨ç⟩ [ts]. ge/j- onsets [ɟ] in yenesta/yunko (barbato_minervini2024:449); creçen stressed on the stem (crescunt), function words e/en/la unstressed.

sentence: Amenos meu senyor, ke bono yed.
ipa: aˈmenos ˈmew seˈɲoɾ ˈke ˈbono ˈjed
gloss_en: Let us love my lord, for he is good.
notes: bono 'good' (H24) non-diphthongized (barbato_minervini2024:158); senyor ⟨ny⟩ [ɲ]; ke < quid; adstrate-free Romance clause closing the set.
